paragraphs.body: In the article, “How the Reality TV Bloom is Affecting Our Mental Health,” Bianca O’Neill disputes that while reality television has been a significantly cultural catalyst, it can lead to harmful societal behaviors. She concurs her point by developing several important ideas. First, O’Neill addresses a study of how many viewers typically watched shows that aligned with their value systems, allowing them to become enthralled within bizarre themes a show may bring. O’Neill herself writes, “if status and self-importance is essential to our self, we will find pleasure in watching those we deem lower in status.” O'Neill is insisting that many viewers view content for the sole purpose of self-gratification in a department someone feels they have superiority over another. Next, she notes research in which one in four viewers' self-worth, physically and emotionally, becomes questioned after watching certain reality programs. Finally, O’Neill highlights the misrepresentation of character, or lack of representation thereof, that the media exudes regarding participants in these shows and the negative impact this may have on viewers or participants.

paragraphs.body: Throughout the article, Oniell expresses how the sudden uprising of reality television has in fact depleted certain aspects of viewers mental wellness and fed into a dangerous world of social media. I always found this quite peculiar, as I wondered how this trend is even so apparent. Can’t people simply detach themselves from these said programs? Truly, I never understood any other way of solving this rising issue. Maybe that’s really why my sophomore year of high school was a complete blur. Maybe that’s really why reality television filled in a void that I never even knew was truly gone nor existed. During November through the end of December in sophomore year, 2021, I had lost both of my grandparents only a couple weeks apart. The second news had gotten to my mother, she took my oldest sister along on the closest flight to Peru, where my grandparents lived. That left me, my two dogs, and dad alone at home. Though, my dad took on longer shifts bartending during the day and during the night, leaving me with a lot of responsibilities nothing could have prepared me for. Not only was taking care of my dogs quite stressful, but I also had schoolwork, exams, projects, and so much more to focus on. All the meals? I had prepared myself if my dad didn’t have time to make one before a shift. Anything that had to do with the house was relied on my then only fifteen-year-old hands. But every night, I always ended it with an episode of Dance Moms.

annotations.text: Maybe that’s really why my sophomore year of high school was a complete blur. Maybe that’s really why reality television filled in a void that I never even knew was truly gone nor existed.
the article, Oniell expresses how the sudden uprising of reality television has in fact depleted certain aspects of viewers mental wellness and fed into a dangerous world of social media.

paragraphs.body: Dance Moms is a show about young girls in a dancing studio, where their mothers get into arguments and fights about how their daughters all deserve some chance in the spotlight. I often found myself getting lost in each episode, laughing or crying or even getting mad at certain people on screen. No matter how stressed I was during the day, I found some semblance in whatever events would take place in every episode. The second the bubble would pop up to a new episode, I would huff as I knew that was my sign to go to bed. Sure, it saddened me a bit, but it made me excited to get through the next day. I always went through this cycle of wondering how I got so hooked on the lives of random little girls, parents fighting over them, absolute mayhem. O’Neill recalls Clinical Psychologist, Dr. Hayley Watson, stating “this releases hormones in our brain that suppress other, less pleasant emotions.” Basically, Watson implies that watching a show that gives us some form of emotion like happiness can cause us to become more addicted to that feeling like a drug. That also means that people use this as a way to overcompensate for a possible lack of that certain emotion in real time. Replaying how often I found myself relating to these girls, whether it was not feeling good enough in a dance or feeling as if they disappointed their other team members, I realize suppression and projection were my reasons. In a way, I tried to imagine myself in these girls; to hear their own mothers and other friends tell them they’re going to be okay. I imagined that I was the one they were telling this to. I would constantly rewatch episodes where the group would win their national numbers, as an effort to suppress the loneliness and grief I did feel when the tv wasn’t on. It became correspondent to how my own mental wellness was starting to crack without watching at least one episode.

paragraphs.body: Throughout the seasons, the girls get a bit older. They had gained their own voices, often giving their opinions, even though the reactions weren’t taken too well. I smiled along, seeing how the little girls I always related to grew up to find confidence in speaking up for themselves. I yearned for this, and as I stayed in my constant battle at home, trying to maintain some order while half the adults were flying in and out of the country, some peace remained. In the midst of all the crazy that was occurring, these very real people on my screen existed and, in my head, could see the emotions I was feeling during every episode in those very events. On the other hand, I never truly was able to live in that moment of processing the loss of my grandparents and a multitude of responsibilities.

annotations.text: On the other hand, I never truly was able to live in that moment of processing the loss of my grandparents and a multitude of responsibilities.

paragraphs.body: In response to Bianca O’Neill’s article, I do believe the boom in reality television can be mentally damaging, but in a way, it had allowed me to escape the insanity I was feeling in my real life. This paradox had only left me to realize that it truly does depend on the individual's experience when watching a show and what point in their life they’re at. While yes, there are much healthier ways to go about healing and processing emotions, but reality television allowed me to express myself and expunge more depressive emotions every night with a simple touch of a play button.

Works Cited “How the Reality TV Boom Is Affecting Our Mental Health.” Rolling Stone Australia, 27 July 2023, au.rollingstone.com/tv/tv-features/reality-tv-affecting-mental-health-48925/#:~:text=%E2%80%9CSeeing%20scenarios%20that%20depict%20real%20life%20brings %20up.
